concise:
Ayah 1–14 · linked video: 01. Saba (Ayah 1-14)

Surah Saba: Commentary on Ayahs 1-14

Ayah 1

ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ

This ayah marks a significant transition as we return to the Makkan surahs. From Surah Saba through Surah Muhammad (approximately Surah 34 to 47), we encounter a sustained sequence of Makkan revelation. To appreciate the context, it’s helpful to understand the chronological order of the major Madani surahs: Baqarah came first, then Anfal, followed by Ali Imran, then Nisa, then Ahzab (which we just studied), then Mumtahinah, and finally Maidah as one of the last major Madani revelations.

Surah Saba and the following Surah Fatir hold a special distinction as the final surahs in the Quranic sequence that begin with الْحَمْدُ. The Quran began with this phrase in Surah Fatiha, establishing praise as the foundation of our relationship with Allah ﷻ. Earlier, we encountered this opening in Surah An’am, then in the middle of the Quran with Surah Kahf. Now, spaced approximately seven years apart in revelation, these final two “hamd surahs” appear together, reminding us that this Ummah is uniquely identified with praise and gratitude.

The Prophet ﷺ himself embodied this identity. He said, “On the Day of Judgment, the flag of hamd will be in my hands.” We are the nation of Alhamdulillah. In every single prayer, we recite الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ. This is our defining characteristic, and the Quran’s identity begins with this same declaration.

Allah ﷻ says: All praise and gratitude belongs exclusively to the One who owns whatever is in the skies and whatever is in the earth. The emphasis on ownership (لَهُۥ - “to Him”) establishes that everything we might praise in creation ultimately belongs to Him. We don’t praise the creation independently; we recognize that all good comes from the Owner of all things.

وَلَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ - And to Him belongs all praise in the afterlife. This is profound because in this life, people praise many things: they praise their leaders, their wealth, their achievements, their heroes. But in the Akhirah, all of that falls away. The only One worthy of praise, the only One who will be praised, is Allah ﷻ. Every tongue will acknowledge this reality.

The ayah concludes: وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ - And He is the All-Wise, the All-Aware. Al-Hakeem means His wisdom is ultimate and complete. Al-Khabeer means His knowledge encompasses every detail, every hidden reality. These names set the foundation for what follows: Allah ﷻ knows what we cannot see, and His wisdom governs all that happens.

Ayah 2

يَعْلَمُ مَا يَلِجُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا يَخْرُجُ مِنْهَا وَمَا يَنزِلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا يَعْرُجُ فِيهَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلرَّحِيمُ ٱلْغَفُورُ

Allah ﷻ knows what goes into the earth and what comes out of it. On the surface level, this refers to seeds going into the earth and plants emerging from it. Rain water penetrates the soil, and vegetation springs forth. But there’s a deeper layer of meaning here that connects directly to the theme of resurrection.

Just as seeds are planted in the earth and we wait expectantly for life to emerge, human beings are also returned to the earth. When we bury someone, we tend to think of it as an ending, but Allah ﷻ is teaching us to see it as a beginning. The grave is like planting a seed. We place the body in the earth, and at the appointed time, when Allah’s command descends like rain from the sky, that person will emerge into their real, eternal life.

This completely changes our perspective on death. A seed going into the ground represents hope and future growth, not loss. Similarly, a believer being buried should be viewed with this same understanding: this is not the end of their life but the beginning of their true, everlasting life in the Akhirah.

وَمَا يَنزِلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا يَعْرُجُ فِيهَا - And He knows what comes down from the sky and what rises up into it. Again, there are multiple dimensions. Physically, rain descends and vapor ascends. But spiritually, so much more is happening. Revelation descends from the sky, angels bringing Allah’s mercy and commands come down. And what rises up? The ruh (soul) travels upward at death. The angels ascend carrying our deeds. Every night when we sleep, our souls journey upward in a minor form of death, as the Quran tells us: اللَّهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِهَا - “Allah takes the souls at the time of their death.”

Now notice something remarkable about how this ayah concludes: وَهُوَ ٱلرَّحِيمُ ٱلْغَفُورُ - And He is the Most Merciful, the Most Forgiving.

This is the only place in the entire Quran where Allah’s mercy (Ar-Raheem) is mentioned before His forgiveness (Al-Ghafoor). Over seventy times in the Quran, the pattern is reversed: forgiveness first, then mercy. Why this unique sequence here?

The answer lies in the imagery itself. When a person is dying, when they are about to go into the earth, what do they desperately need? Mercy. The connections of rahm (the womb, family ties, worldly relationships) are being severed. At that moment of transition, they call out for Allah’s rahma, His mercy. This is why Ar-Raheem comes first.

But when that person emerges from the earth on the Day of Judgment, when they come out for the resurrection, what becomes the absolute priority? Forgiveness. On that Day, family connections won’t matter anymore. أَفَمَن يَتَّقِي بِوَجْهِهِ سُوءَ الْعَذَابِ - The ties of kinship will be cut. The only thing that will matter is whether Allah ﷻ forgives you. This is why Al-Ghafoor comes second, in sequence with what rises from the earth.

There’s also a beautiful connection to knowledge. The ayah begins with knowledge (يَعْلَمُ - He knows), and throughout the Quran, knowledge and mercy are frequently paired together. In Surah Ghafir, we read: رَبَّنَا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَّحْمَةً وَعِلْمًا - “Our Lord, You have encompassed all things in mercy and knowledge.” The teacher given special knowledge from Allah in Surah Kahf is described as someone granted رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَعَلَّمْنَاهُ - “mercy from Us and We taught him knowledge.” Even the beautiful name of Surah Ar-Rahman is followed immediately by عَلَّمَ الْقُرْآنَ - “taught the Quran.” Knowledge and mercy flow together.

Since this ayah is dominated by Allah’s knowledge, His universal attribute of mercy naturally takes precedence. But His forgiveness, Al-Maghfirah, is specific. Mercy applies to all of creation—to the ants, the sun, the horses, every creature. But forgiveness is specifically for those who commit wrongs, for those who need to be pardoned. The universal attribute (mercy) is mentioned first, closest to the universal discussion of all things entering and leaving the earth and sky. The specific attribute (forgiveness) is mentioned second, in proximity to what will specifically concern human beings who are accountable for their choices.

Ayah 3

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَا تَأْتِينَا ٱلسَّاعَةُ ۖ قُلْ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّى لَتَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ ۖ لَا يَعْزُبُ عَنْهُ مِثْقَالُ ذَرَّةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَآ أَصْغَرُ مِن ذَٰلِكَ وَلَآ أَكْبَرُ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ

Those who disbelieve say, “The Hour will not come to us.” This is their confident assertion, their denial of resurrection and accountability. But Allah ﷻ immediately commands His Messenger ﷺ to respond with certainty: قُلْ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّى - Say: “On the contrary! By my Lord…” This is one of the rare occasions in the Quran where the Prophet ﷺ is instructed to take an oath by Allah ﷻ Himself, emphasizing the absolute certainty of what follows.

لَتَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ - It will most certainly come to you. The structure here is emphatic, leaving no room for doubt. عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ - By the Knower of the Unseen, the One who knows everything hidden from creation.

Then Allah ﷻ makes an extraordinary statement about the scope of His knowledge: لَا يَعْزُبُ عَنْهُ مِثْقَالُ ذَرَّةٍ - Not even the weight of a speck escapes Him. The word يَعْزُبُ carries multiple meanings that enrich our understanding. It means to be far away, so distant that it’s barely visible, like watching a ship disappear over the horizon. It also means to be hidden away for so long that people stop looking for it, stop even remembering it exists.

Allah ﷻ is saying that مِثْقَالُ ذَرَّةٍ - the weight of the smallest speck, something so tiny you cannot see it even if it’s right in front of you - is not hidden from Him due to distance or due to its insignificance. Think about this: for something to escape our perception, it only needs to be tiny OR far away. A dust particle floating right by your eye goes unseen because of its size. A mountain becomes invisible because of distance. Allah’s knowledge encompasses both dimensions of impossibility for human perception.

فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - Neither in the skies nor in the earth. When Allah mentions “the skies”, this includes what we understand as the physical universe. The Quran tells us that the lowest sky is decorated with stars (وَلَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا السَّمَاءَ الدُّنْيَا بِمَصَابِيحَ), so everything our telescopes can observe—the entire observable universe with all its galaxies—is still just the first of seven skies. Allah’s knowledge encompasses all of this and everything beyond.

وَلَآ أَصْغَرُ مِن ذَٰلِكَ وَلَآ أَكْبَرُ - Nor anything smaller than that, nor anything larger. We thought the atom was the smallest unit, but then we discovered subatomic particles. We found an entire universe exists within the atom itself, with particles in orbit like celestial bodies. Whether it’s the vast expanse of galaxies or the quantum realm of subatomic particles, nothing escapes Allah’s knowledge.

إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ - Except that it is recorded in a clear book. Everything is documented, preserved, known. But why is Allah ﷻ telling us this? What’s the purpose of emphasizing His knowledge of the smallest and largest things?

The answer comes in the next ayah, but we can see the direction: this is about your deeds. Just as Allah ﷻ knows about subatomic particles we can’t even see, He knows about the deeds you think are insignificant. That moment you came home and simply said “I love you, Mom” with genuine affection—recorded. That small act of kindness you didn’t think twice about—preserved. We don’t keep journals of these moments because they seem too small to matter, but Allah ﷻ doesn’t overlook anything.

Notice the emphasis here is on Allah’s mercy and not letting any good deed go unnoticed. Usually when we think about “nothing escapes Allah’s knowledge,” we think about sins being recorded. But in this surah, which began with Ar-Raheem before Al-Ghafoor, the emphasis is on Allah’s mercy not overlooking your smallest good deeds.

Ayah 4

لِّيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ

So that He may fully compensate those who believed and did righteous deeds. This is the purpose of that meticulous record-keeping. Not a single good deed, no matter how small, will be forgotten or left unrewarded.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ - Those people will have forgiveness. Now the word maghfirah (forgiveness) appears, and its placement is significant. Before you can receive Allah’s generous gifts, before you can enter Jannah and enjoy its blessings, you need to be cleared of your wrongs. Forgiveness comes first logically because you can’t enjoy the good life until you’re out of trouble. No one thinks about rewards while they’re in jail; first, you need to be pardoned, then you can think about what comes next.

This is why in the vast majority of the Quran, when Allah ﷻ addresses human beings directly, He mentions forgiveness before mercy. Humans need to be forgiven first, then they can receive mercy. But in the previous ayah, where Allah’s universal knowledge was being discussed—His knowledge of all things in the skies and the earth—His universal attribute of mercy was mentioned first, and His specific attribute of forgiveness second. Now that we’re explicitly talking about believers and their deeds, forgiveness naturally comes first in the sequence of what they’ll receive.

وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ - And a noble, generous provision. Rizq Kareem isn’t just about quantity; it’s about the manner of giving. Kareem means generous, noble, honoring. Anyone can give you a gift, but when someone gives with nobility and honor, it’s different. Sometimes a gift given rudely feels like an insult. Allah ﷻ doesn’t just give us Jannah; He honors us in how He gives it. There’s dignity in the giving itself. This is what Kareem captures—not just “here’s your reward,” but you are honored, elevated, and dignified in the way you receive it.

Ayah 5

وَٱلَّذِينَ سَعَوْ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا مُعَـٰجِزِينَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مِّن رِّجْزٍ أَلِيمٌ

And those who strove against Our signs as adversaries. The word سَعَوْ means they made efforts, they worked hard. Making effort regarding Allah’s ayat is normally praiseworthy—we’re supposed to strive to understand the Quran, to reflect on Allah’s signs. But the crucial qualifier is مُعَـٰجِزِينَ.

The root ع-ج-ز gives us qadara (to be capable) and its opposite ’ajaza (to be incapable). A mu’ajiz is someone trying to make something else fail, to render it incapable of reaching its goal. Imagine you’re building a tower with blocks and someone keeps knocking down the first floor so the whole thing collapses. They’re a mu’ajiz—actively working to prevent your success.

These people see the Quran, see its message, and recognize it’s on a trajectory toward victory. They can’t let that happen. So they work tirelessly to undermine it, to make this project fail, to prevent it from reaching its completion. They try to create an image of the Quran that scares people away from even picking up a copy. They paint a picture of Muslims that makes people terrified at the mere sight of someone who looks Muslim. They engineer scenarios designed to ensure da’wah will fail.

If they succeed in stopping Allah’s message, they would be called mu’jizeen. But they are called mu’ajizeen instead—those who are trying but will not succeed. Near the end of the Prophet’s ﷺ life, in one of the last surahs revealed (around the time of Surah Maidah), Allah ﷻ declared: وَمَا أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ - “You will never be able to overpower Allah.” You thought you might stop His mission, but you won’t.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مِّن رِّجْزٍ أَلِيمٌ - Those people will have a punishment from rijz that is painful. Rijz means disturbance, agitation, terror. It’s used for poetry chanted during wartime to strike fear into hearts. It’s the evil whispers of Shaytan that create nightmares, the deep terror a child feels imagining something in the closet. Their punishment will be composed of this element of terror—a painful, disturbing, deeply unsettling torment.

Ayah 6

وَيَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقَّ وَيَهْدِىٓ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ

And those who have been given knowledge see clearly that what has been revealed to you from your Lord is the truth. This is describing people from the People of the Book, scholars who possess real knowledge of previous scriptures. When they encounter the Quran, they immediately recognize its qualities and authenticity.

وَيَهْدِىٓ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ - And it guides to the path of the All-Mighty, the Praiseworthy. Al-Azeez is the One who has ultimate authority, who cannot be overpowered. Al-Hameed is the One who deserves all praise, even without anyone praising Him. The Quran guides not just to any path, but specifically to the path that leads to knowing and connecting with the One who has all power and who is worthy of all praise.

Ayah 7

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ هَلْ نَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ يُنَبِّئُكُمْ إِذَا مُزِّقْتُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ إِنَّكُمْ لَفِى خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ

Those who disbelieved said—and here Allah ﷻ gives us a scene from Makkah. Picture some people sitting together, perhaps drinking, casually talking. The conversation turns to the news around town.

هَلْ نَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ - “Should we tell you about this guy?” There’s a man who يُنَبِّئُكُمْ - wants to inform you, claims to have news for you. And what does he say?

إِذَا مُزِّقْتُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ - That when you have been completely torn to shreds. The word مُزِّقَ comes from tearing cloth violently, ripping it to pieces. In our time, imagine putting paper through a shredder—that’s the image. They’re describing what happens to the human body in the grave: eaten by the creatures of the earth, bones deteriorated, body parts scattered and decomposed beyond recognition.

إِنَّكُمْ لَفِى خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ - He says you’re going to be in a brand new creation. You’ll be put back together, fresh and whole. “Can you believe this guy is saying stuff like that?”

The mockery in their tone is clear. They’re ridiculing the very idea of resurrection, treating it as absurd.

Ayah 8

أَفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَم بِهِۦ جِنَّةٌۢ ۗ بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ وَٱلضَّلَـٰلِ ٱلْبَعِيدِ

Then they start speculating about what could explain this behavior. أَفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا - “Did he make up a lie against Allah?” But that doesn’t fit. They know Muhammad ﷺ. These are the same people who gave him the title As-Sadiq (the Truthful) and Al-Ameen (the Trustworthy). They’re the scoundrels of society, yet even they recognized his unimpeachable honesty. When they had disputes they couldn’t resolve among themselves, they’d take their problems to him to arbitrate because they trusted him more than they trusted each other.

So they think, “Could he really fabricate a lie against Allah Himself? That doesn’t match who he is.”

أَم بِهِۦ جِنَّةٌۢ - “Or maybe he’s possessed by jinn? Poor guy, maybe he’s lost his mind.” They’re trying to find some explanation, some hypothesis that makes sense of why Muhammad ﷺ would say something they find so incredible.

But Allah ﷻ cuts through their speculation with the truth: بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ - Rather, those who don’t believe in the afterlife are already in punishment.

This is profound. The surah opened with Allah’s mercy, His love, His compassion. But you cannot truly understand love, mercy, and compassion without understanding the Akhirah. If you don’t believe in the afterlife, you cannot grasp what Allah’s mercy really means.

Think of a child raised in an orphanage without love, without mercy, without compassion. Isn’t that child being raised in a form of torture? That emotional deprivation is itself painful torment. We know from psychology that emotional pain can far exceed physical pain. When someone you love scolds you harshly, you might say, “Please, just hit me instead—I can’t bear those words.” Emotional pain cuts deeper.

Someone without belief in the Akhirah lives in this kind of torture. Everything Allah ﷻ puts them through in life feels purposeless, random, cruel. Why did this happen to me? Why didn’t I get that? What kind of God would allow this? Why did my marriage fail? Why were my parents abusive? Why did I lose my family? Why did I get sick? The questions pile up with no satisfying answers, creating constant frustration and pain.

They don’t understand that this life is just one small chapter, that the real life—the one you’re earning through these tests—begins when you’re planted back in the earth like a seed. This temporary life is just preparation. Allah ﷻ called this life لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ - just entertainment and distraction compared to the real life.

Without that perspective, without understanding Allah’s mercy through the lens of eternal compensation and justice, people live in misery regardless of their worldly success. This explains why surveys found that one of the wealthiest counties in America had among the highest suicide rates. You have everything everyone else wants—mansions, fame, status, beauty—yet you’re depressed, unable to sleep without pills, wanting to escape reality through drugs and alcohol.

Why are the clubs filled with people desperate to forget their lives, to be around strangers rather than family? Because when the Akhirah is gone from a person’s consciousness, they can’t understand Allah’s mercy. They live in torture no matter what they’ve enjoyed in this world.

وَٱلضَّلَـٰلِ ٱلْبَعِيدِ - And in faraway misguidance. The old translations say “in error far astray,” but the meaning is simpler: they are completely lost. Here they are talking about how confused Muhammad ﷺ supposedly is, yet they are the ones who are utterly, hopelessly lost. The irony is stark.

Ayah 9

أَفَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِن نَّشَأْ نَخْسِفْ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ أَوْ نُسْقِطْ عَلَيْهِمْ كِسَفًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُّنِيبٍ

Have they not observed what is in front of them and what is behind them of the sky and the earth? Look around at this planet, at the universe. إِن نَّشَأْ نَخْسِفْ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ - If We wanted, We could make the earth swallow them. The word نَخْسِفْ suggests the earth opening its mouth and consuming them, as if the earth itself doesn’t want to hold these filthy people.

This imagery connects to the Day of Judgment, when the Quran describes the earth giving out its burdens (وَأَخْرَجَتِ الْأَرْضُ أَثْقَالَهَا). It’s as if the earth has been holding something bad-tasting in its mouth and finally spits it out, relieved to be rid of the load.

أَوْ نُسْقِطْ عَلَيْهِمْ كِسَفًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ - Or We could drop pieces of the sky upon them—a meteor, a mountain, something massive falling from above.

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُّنِيبٍ - Indeed in that is a sign for every slave who turns back. The word مُنِيب� (muneeb) comes from inabah, meaning a spiritual turning back, a slave’s heart turning back to the Master. For anyone whose heart is inclined to return to Allah ﷻ, the sky and the earth are enough. You don’t need anything more than to look around at creation to find your way back.

Ayah 10

۞ وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ مِنَّا فَضْلًا ۖ يَـٰجِبَالُ أَوِّبِى مَعَهُۥ وَٱلطَّيْرَ ۖ وَأَلَنَّا لَهُ ٱلْحَدِيدَ

Now comes a remarkable transition. Speaking of a slave who wants to turn back, one of the greatest examples was Dawud (AS). And certainly We gave to Dawud from Us a special favor.

يَـٰجِبَالُ أَوِّبِى مَعَهُۥ - “O mountains! Sing along with him.” The word أَوِّبِى comes from the same root as muneeb—returning, coming back. In the context of singing, it refers to the chorus that returns over and over. Dawud (AS) would walk through valleys, mountains surrounding him, and when he reached the chorus of his songs of praise, the entire mountains would vibrate with his voice, singing along with him.

وَٱلطَّيْرَ - And the birds. They would join in too, their chirping becoming part of the symphony of praise.

Picture this: Dawud (AS) walking through nature, and all of creation participating in his worship. The mountains echo his voice, the birds harmonize with him. This might sound like a scene from a cartoon, all gentle and soft. But then Allah ﷻ immediately reminds us of who Dawud (AS) really was:

وَأَلَنَّا لَهُ ٱلْحَدِيدَ - And We made iron soft for him. The word لَيِّن means soft like a fresh date. Some scholars say Allah ﷻ gave Dawud (AS) the ability to melt iron. Others say something more remarkable: when Dawud (AS) would hold metal in his hands, he could mold it like putty, shaping it however he wanted.

So before you think he’s just singing and frolicking with nature, understand that this is the same man who could take your sword and bend it into a noodle with his bare hands. This was his strength, his power.

Ayah 11

أَنِ ٱعْمَلْ سَـٰبِغَـٰتٍ وَقَدِّرْ فِى ٱلسَّرْدِ ۖ وَٱعْمَلُوا۟ صَـٰلِحًا ۖ إِنِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ

Make coats of armor. The word سَـٰبِغَـٰت refers to wide, protective armor. Earlier in the Quran, we saw Nuh (AS) being taught the engineering of shipbuilding by revelation. Now we see Dawud (AS) being taught by revelation how to create defensive armor.

Human beings figured out how to make weapons on their own—swords and spears came from human innovation. But defense? The protective equipment? That came from Allah’s teaching. Everything we have now in terms of defensive armor, bulletproof vests and protective gear, traces back to this original revelation given to Dawud (AS).

وَقَدِّرْ فِى ٱلسَّرْدِ - And be precise in the weaving. A soldier has a helmet and body armor, but the neck remains exposed and vulnerable. Allah ﷻ taught Dawud (AS) to create a chainmail-like covering for the neck, weaving it together so precisely that no links would be loose, ensuring complete protection.

Make sure it’s tight, make sure it’s perfect. Be a perfectionist in your craft. This is one level of meaning in قَدِّر.

But there’s another dimension: وَقَدِّرْ can also mean “measure it out”—don’t overdo it. Even though you’re excellent at this craft and you could keep making more and more armor, hold back. Know when to stop. Just because you excel at a career doesn’t mean you should be obsessive-compulsive about it, working 85 hours a week, neglecting your family, abandoning your spiritual growth, taking on every extra project to climb the corporate ladder.

This advice applies universally. The physician who thinks, “If I just put in a few more hours, attend a few more conferences, I’ll become department head”—this person needs to hear: قَدِّرْ فِى ٱلسَّرْدِ. Take it easy. Hold back. There’s more to life than career advancement.

So it’s both: be excellent and precise in your work, but also know your limits. Don’t let work consume your entire existence.

وَٱعْمَلُوا۟ صَـٰلِحًا - And all of you act righteously. Now Allah ﷻ shifts from speaking to Dawud (AS) alone to addressing the entire army. They’re developing this military technology that gives them a significant advantage. They’re thinking, “Who can challenge us now? We’re untouchable.”

But Allah ﷻ warns them: You better watch your behavior. This power isn’t being given to you so you can wreak havoc on earth. Control yourselves.

This is crucial because historically, victorious armies commit some of the worst atrocities. When an army conquers, there’s no police to stop them—they are the police. There’s no government to restrain them—they are the government. It’s still a state of war, so “anything goes,” and they can get away with the most heinous crimes.

Allah ﷻ is specifically warning them: Now that you have this advantage that virtually guarantees your military success, be even more careful about your character and conduct.

إِنِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ - Indeed I am, regarding everything you do, fully watchful. Everything is in plain view before Allah ﷻ.

We’re being told about Dawud (AS) and the military might he was given. Remember, he had already demonstrated remarkable skill as a youth when he killed Jalut (Goliath) with a slingshot, as mentioned in Surah Baqarah. Jalut was wearing heavy armor, completely covered except for his eyes, and was so massive you needed to be huge just to walk around in that much metal. Dawud (AS) hit him right between the eyes with a stone, straight through to the brain, and when that giant fell, the entire enemy army’s morale collapsed. That was the beginning of Dawud’s victories.

Now he’s been given even more ability—the power to mold iron itself. His son Sulayman (AS) inherited this kingdom and took on his father’s life work. What was Sulayman (AS) given? We see in the next ayah.

Ayah 12

وَلِسُلَيْمَـٰنَ ٱلرِّيحَ غُدُوُّهَا شَهْرٌ وَرَوَاحُهَا شَهْرٌ ۖ وَأَسَلْنَا لَهُۥ عَيْنَ ٱلْقِطْرِ ۖ وَمِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ مَن يَعْمَلُ بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِۦ ۖ وَمَن يَزِغْ مِنْهُمْ عَنْ أَمْرِنَا نُذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ

And for Sulayman, the wind. غُدُوُّهَا شَهْرٌ - Its morning journey would be a month. What would take anyone else a month of travel by land, Sulayman (AS) could cover in one morning. The morning wouldn’t even be over and he’d arrive at his destination.

وَرَوَاحُهَا شَهْرٌ - And its evening journey, a month. Imagine: Maghrib is about to begin and he’s in some distant land. He tells his family, “I’ll be home in ten minutes,” and before the sun sets, the wind has carried him back home. No traffic, no delays—just the wind following his command.

This is essentially the airline industry thousands of years before airplanes. Even today, what takes a month by road at 70 miles per hour would take far less time by air. But back then, without modern vehicles, travel was much slower. Sulayman (AS) was given control over transportation in a way that defied all normal limitations.

So one messenger was given control over military might, and the other over transportation. Both of these are essential for any military operation. When armies can’t transport their supplies—when routes are cut off by weather or terrain—entire campaigns collapse. The German invasion of Russia in World War II faltered significantly because frozen conditions prevented supply lines from functioning.

وَأَسَلْنَا لَهُۥ عَيْنَ ٱلْقِطْرِ - And We melted for him springs of copper. His father was given iron; he was given copper. Not just a little bit—springs of molten copper flowing for him.

Picture the kind of factory operation this requires. You need massive amounts of heat, industrial-scale work, laborers who can handle extremely high temperatures. Where do you find workers who can work with molten metal? Well, there’s one workforce that might be perfect: creatures made of fire.

وَمِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ مَن يَعْمَلُ بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِۦ - And among the jinn were those who worked right in front of him by the permission of his Lord. Allah ﷻ emphasizes: بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِۦ - by the permission of his Master. This is being mentioned to the Quraysh because in their theology, they believed their idols had special powers. They worshiped these stones thinking they possessed supernatural abilities.

Allah ﷻ is telling them: You think those powerless rocks are special? Let Me tell you about real prophets and real power. Mountains sang alongside one. Another controlled the wind and commanded jinn. If you want to worship power, these prophets would be far more worthy. But notice—everything they had was بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِۦ, by Allah’s permission. They had no independent power. It all came from their Master.

وَمَن يَزِغْ مِنْهُمْ عَنْ أَمْرِنَا نُذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ - And whoever among them deviated from Our command, We would make him taste the punishment of the blazing fire.

The jinn are beings with free will. They have a shaytan-like nature, an inclination toward rebellion. So there were consequences in place: step out of line, and you’ll taste the punishment of hellfire. A famous scholar in India was once challenged: “How can jinn be thrown in hellfire when they’re made of fire? Fire won’t hurt them. Your religion has a flaw.”

The scholar picked up some dirt and threw it in the questioner’s eyes. The man recoiled: “What did you do that for?!”

The scholar replied: “You’re made of dirt. Did the dirt hurt?”

“Oh. I understand now.”

Just because they’re made of fire doesn’t mean fire can’t harm them. Different fire, different intensity, different reality.

Ayah 13

يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُۥ مَا يَشَآءُ مِن مَّحَـٰرِيبَ وَتَمَـٰثِيلَ وَجِفَانٍ كَٱلْجَوَابِ وَقُدُورٍ رَّاسِيَـٰتٍ ۚ ٱعْمَلُوٓا۟ ءَالَ دَاوُۥدَ شُكْرًا ۚ وَقَلِيلٌ مِّنْ عِبَادِىَ ٱلشَّكُورُ

They would make for him whatever he wished. مِن مَّحَـٰرِيبَ - Huge castles, fortifications. But مَّحَـٰرِيب also means dedicated places of worship. He didn’t just have them build government offices and palaces; he had them construct spaces where people could worship Allah ﷻ.

وَتَمَـٰثِيلَ - And statues, huge monuments. These were carved from rock, massive structures. This was before such monuments became haram in Islamic law. The law that prohibits statues and images came later, but at that time, creating these monumental structures was permissible.

وَجِفَانٍ كَٱلْجَوَابِ - And dishes like ponds. These were enormous serving dishes, so large they resembled ponds. Why so huge? Because of how much charity Sulayman (AS) distributed. They would need to set up ladders against these giant containers to serve food to the poor. Imagine the soup kitchen operation—massive pots with food being ladled out to feed countless people.

وَقُدُورٍ رَّاسِيَـٰتٍ - And firmly anchored cooking pots. قُدُور is larger than جِفَان—these were enormous cauldrons. رَّاسِيَـٰت means pegged into the ground, so heavy they couldn’t be moved. They became permanent fixtures because of their massive size.

ٱعْمَلُوٓا۟ ءَالَ دَاوُۥدَ شُكْرًا - Work, O family of Dawud, in gratitude. Allah ﷻ calls out to the entire family: this is your purpose, to work in service of people out of gratitude to Allah ﷻ. Building these structures, feeding the poor, serving creation—this is how you show thanks.

We’re learning that all of this construction, all of this power and wealth, was directed toward serving people. The motivation behind it was شُكْر—gratitude to Allah ﷻ.

وَقَلِيلٌ مِّنْ عِبَادِىَ ٱلشَّكُورُ - And how few of My slaves are the ones who are deeply, constantly grateful. قَلِيل already means few, but ٱلشَّكُورُ intensifies it further. الشَّكُور shares a root with cleanliness—to be pure, clean, refined. It’s an intensive form: not just grateful occasionally, but someone whose entire orientation is gratitude, who maintains constant thankfulness.

This is incredibly rare. Most of Allah’s slaves don’t reach this level of profound, enduring gratitude.

Ayah 14

فَلَمَّا قَضَيْنَا عَلَيْهِ ٱلْمَوْتَ مَا دَلَّهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَوْتِهِۦٓ إِلَّا دَآبَّةُ ٱلْأَرْضِ تَأْكُلُ مِنسَأَتَهُۥ ۖ فَلَمَّا خَرَّ تَبَيَّنَتِ ٱلْجِنُّ أَن لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ ٱلْغَيْبَ مَا لَبِثُوا۟ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْمُهِينِ

Then when We decreed death upon him—meaning Sulayman (AS)—nothing indicated his death to them except دَآبَّةُ ٱلْأَرْضِ, a creature of the earth, تَأْكُلُ مِنسَأَتَهُۥ, eating away at his staff.

The scene: Sulayman (AS) is on his throne, leaning on his staff, watching the jinn work. He’s overseeing the entire operation. Allah ﷻ takes his soul, but his body remains in that position—leaning on his staff, appearing to watch them.

The jinn keep working. Days pass. Weeks. Months. They glance up occasionally and think, “Wow, he’s really in a serious mood today. He hasn’t said a word. Better keep working.” They’re afraid to make eye contact, afraid to break their focus, because when he’s watching them this intently, they know what happens if they slack off. So they work and work and work.

Meanwhile, termites are eating away at his staff. مِنسَأَة is the staff, the stick he’s leaning on. Termites—دَآبَّةُ ٱلْأَرْضِ, creatures that consume everything and return it to earth—are gradually destroying the staff’s integrity.

Finally, the staff gives way. خَرَّ - He fell to the ground.

فَلَمَّا خَرَّ تَبَيَّنَتِ ٱلْجِنُّ - When he fell, it became clear to the jinn أَن لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ ٱلْغَيْبَ - that had they known the unseen مَا لَبِثُوا۟ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْمُهِينِ - they would not have remained in that humiliating punishment.

The jinn had always tried to present themselves as knowers of the unseen. People who practice magic, who try to communicate with jinn, seek them out precisely because they think jinn have access to hidden knowledge. But Allah ﷻ used this incident to expose the truth: he died right in front of their eyes and they had no idea.

If they truly knew the unseen, they would have known when his soul departed and they wouldn’t have continued working in that humiliating labor. There are levels of the unseen. The jinn exist in the unseen realm—that’s level one. But there are levels two, three, four and beyond that are completely beyond their access, highly classified information far above their clearance. They thought there were no higher levels, but Allah ﷻ showed them: there’s much more you don’t know.

This completes the account of the kingdom on one end of the Arabian Peninsula—the Palestinian side where Dawud and Sulayman (AS) ruled with their God-given might and technology. We’ve seen their power, their construction projects, their service to people, and now the reality of how even the greatest of kingdoms end. All of this is setting up what comes next in the surah, where we’ll see the other end of the Arabian Peninsula and learn how the Quraysh came to hold their position in the middle.

Ayah 15–27 · linked video: 04. Saba (Ayah 15-27)

Surah Saba: Ayahs 15-27

Ayah 15

لَقَدْ كَانَ لِسَبَإٍ فِى مَسْكَنِهِمْ ءَايَةٌ ۖ جَنَّتَانِ عَن يَمِينٍ وَشِمَالٍ ۖ كُلُوا۟ مِن رِّزْقِ رَبِّكُمْ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لَهُۥ ۚ بَلْدَةٌ طَيِّبَةٌ وَرَبٌّ غَفُورٌ

Allah ﷻ now takes us on a historical tour of the Kingdom of Saba, located in Yemen. He says that for the people of Saba, in their very dwellings and homes, there was an آيَة - a miracle, a sign. What was this sign? جَنَّتَانِ عَن يَمِينٍ وَشِمَالٍ - gardens on the right and left. This means that both sides, all territories of their kingdom, were flooded with greenery. The entire landscape was covered in lush vegetation.

Allah ﷻ then tells us what was said to them: كُلُوا۟ مِن رِّزْقِ رَبِّكُمْ - “Consume from the provision of your Master.” وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لَهُۥ - “And be grateful to Him.” This is a divine commentary on their situation. Allah ﷻ describes what they enjoyed: بَلْدَةٌ طَيِّبَةٌ - “a good and pure land,” وَرَبٌّ غَفُورٌ - “and a Master that is exceedingly forgiving.”

The significance of this phrase is powerful. You have been given everything - a blessed land, abundant provision, prosperity on all sides. You’re supposed to be grateful, but you’re not. And despite your ingratitude, your Master has been continually forgiving to you. All these mercies have been granted to you, and you haven’t taken advantage of them.

This presents a stark contrast within the same surah. On one side of the Arabian Peninsula, you have Sulaiman (AS) and Dawud (AS), grateful for the great things they enjoyed. On the other side, in Yemen, you have the people of Saba - ungrateful despite being given similar or even greater material blessings.

Ayah 16

فَأَعْرَضُوا۟ فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ سَيْلَ ٱلْعَرِمِ وَبَدَّلْنَـٰهُم بِجَنَّتَيْهِمْ جَنَّتَيْنِ ذَوَاتَىْ أُكُلٍ خَمْطٍ وَأَثْلٍ وَشَىْءٍ مِّن سِدْرٍ قَلِيلٍ

فَأَعْرَضُوا۟ - “Then they ignored.” The object isn’t mentioned because it’s obvious - they ignored the command to be grateful for what Allah ﷻ had given them.

فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ سَيْلَ ٱلْعَرِمِ - “So We sent against them the flood of al-’Arim.” العَرِم refers to a dam. This was essentially a city built below sea level, protected by a massive dam system. When the dam cracked and the levees broke, the entire city was flooded and destroyed.

وَبَدَّلْنَـٰهُم بِجَنَّتَيْهِمْ جَنَّتَيْنِ - “And We replaced their gardens with just two kinds of gardens.” Notice the devastating irony - they used to have multiple gardens covering all their territories. Now Allah ﷻ mentions just two types of vegetation that replaced everything they had.

ذَوَاتَىْ أُكُلٍ خَمْطٍ - The word أُكُل refers to things that can be eaten, but خَمْطٍ means sour and tasteless - food that a person doesn’t like to eat, that provides no enjoyment or satisfaction.

وَأَثْلٍ - Tamarisk trees, the kind of hardy plant that only grows in desert climates among rocks, with thorny, spiky leaves. These don’t bear fruit and don’t provide much benefit to human beings.

وَشَىْءٍ مِّن سِدْرٍ قَلِيلٍ - “And some lotus plants, just a little bit here and there.” What used to be completely covered in greenery now has some green scattered about, and even that kind of vegetation is not something human beings can get much benefit from. It’s not really a garden anymore in any meaningful sense.

This was catastrophic because Saba was both a trade capital and an agricultural capital. They used to export vast amounts of their agricultural produce and transport it inland throughout the region. After this flood, which occurred approximately 120 years before the birth of the Prophet ﷺ (around 450 years after ’Isa [AS]), they were completely wiped out economically.

Ayah 17

ذَٰلِكَ جَزَيْنَـٰهُم بِمَا كَفَرُوا۟ ۖ وَهَلْ نُجَـٰزِىٓ إِلَّا ٱلْكَفُورَ

ذَٰلِكَ جَزَيْنَـٰهُم بِمَا كَفَرُوا۟ - “That is how We compensated them on account of their disbelief.” The word جَزَيْنَـٰهُم comes from جَزَاء, which carries the meaning of taking revenge or giving full recompense.

وَهَلْ نُجَـٰزِىٓ إِلَّا ٱلْكَفُورَ - “And do We take such severe revenge except against the one who is extremely ungrateful?” The word الْكَفُورَ - the intensified form - indicates someone who is extremely, persistently ungrateful.

By phrasing it this way, Allah ﷻ is telling us that this nation wasn’t just occasionally ungrateful. They were profoundly and persistently ungrateful, and that’s why such severe consequences befell them.

Ayah 18

وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ وَبَيْنَ ٱلْقُرَى ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا قُرًى ظَـٰهِرَةً وَقَدَّرْنَا فِيهَا ٱلسَّيْرَ ۖ سِيرُوا۟ فِيهَا لَيَالِىَ وَأَيَّامًا ءَامِنِينَ

Before their destruction, Allah ﷻ gives us insight into their economic situation and what led to their downfall.

وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ وَبَيْنَ ٱلْقُرَى ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا - “And We had placed between them and between the towns that We had blessed…” The towns that Allah ﷻ blessed refer to Jerusalem and the Palestinian region, which represented another major economic center.

قُرًى ظَـٰهِرَةً - “obvious towns” or “visible towns.” Between Saba in Yemen and the blessed lands of Palestine, there weren’t just vast empty deserts. There were actually many smaller towns and cities in between. This geography played a crucial role in trade because these weren’t isolated economic islands. When you have major civilizations on both ends and smaller towns in the middle, you create an interdependent economy where everyone benefits.

If you’re trying to sell to the far-end city, and there are all these other settlements in the middle, you’re sharing the economy with them. These smaller towns weren’t taking away from sales - they were part of a network of prosperity. You can think of it in modern terms as the relationship between large corporations and small local businesses.

وَقَدَّرْنَا فِيهَا ٱلسَّيْرَ - “And We had made travel through these towns very easy.” The travel was accessible and manageable for people of different means.

سِيرُوا۟ فِيهَا لَيَالِىَ وَأَيَّامًا ءَامِنِينَ - “Travel through them by night and day, safely.” Unlike the dangerous conditions that characterized much of Arabia, where travelers constantly faced threats, these routes were secure. You could travel at any time - night or day - and remain safe. This was a special blessing that enabled widespread economic participation.

Ayah 19

فَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا بَـٰعِدْ بَيْنَ أَسْفَارِنَا وَظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ وَمَزَّقْنَـٰهُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ

But the wealthy elite of Saba saw this shared prosperity differently. فَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا بَـٰعِدْ بَيْنَ أَسْفَارِنَا - “They said: Our Master, cause distance between our journeys.”

What were they asking for? They wanted the journey to become so difficult that only the very wealthy would be able to make the investment required to travel all the way to the other end of the territory. Allah ﷻ had made travel easy, which meant everybody was getting economic opportunity. The small businessman could participate in trade too, and they were succeeding. The elite didn’t want that to happen.

They wanted the roads and the journey to become so challenging that only the extremely wealthy could engage in long-distance trade. The longer and more difficult the journey, the more capital you need to undertake it. They wanted this economic exchange to happen only between the very wealthy, and they wanted to suffocate the small businesses and towns in the process.

The fact that Allah ﷻ mentions them praying for this reveals something significant. This wasn’t just a business strategy - they made it a religious cause. They gathered people together and framed their economic elitism as a matter of national and religious interest. “Our economy is in danger because of these lesser competitors. We need to protect our prosperity.” Prayer became a means of congregating people and galvanizing support for their agenda.

You’ll notice even today this same phenomenon: groups that claim to be religious while promoting economic policies that harm the vulnerable, that refuse to share prosperity, that want to export but not import, that seek to eliminate competition rather than create space for everyone to thrive. They use religion to unite people behind an agenda of greed. This isn’t new - it’s the old story repeating itself.

وَظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ - “And they wronged themselves.” By making this prayer, by having this attitude, they weren’t harming Allah ﷻ or His creation alone - they were destroying themselves.

فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ - “So We made them into stories,” أَحَادِيثَ - historical tales that people tell. The great nation of Saba became a cautionary tale. People would travel through the region and say, “Did you know what happened to them? Can you believe this was once the prosperous Kingdom of Saba?”

وَمَزَّقْنَـٰهُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ - “And We tore them apart with complete tearing” - they were utterly shattered and dispersed. When this destruction occurred, many Yemenis migrated. The Aws and Khazraj tribes of Medina, for instance, were originally migrants from Yemen. They were drawn to Medina partly because its green landscape reminded them of what they had lost.

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ - “Indeed, in all of that there are miraculous signs for everyone who is extremely patient and extremely grateful.” Patient with what? Patient with what you have. Don’t become consumed by greed. And be truly grateful for what you’ve been given. These are the two qualities that the people of Saba lacked, and these are the qualities that would have saved them.

Ayah 20

وَلَقَدْ صَدَّقَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِبْلِيسُ ظَنَّهُۥ فَٱتَّبَعُوهُ إِلَّا فَرِيقًا مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Now Allah ﷻ reveals the spiritual reality behind their downfall: وَلَقَدْ صَدَّقَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِبْلِيسُ ظَنَّهُۥ - “And certainly Iblis confirmed his assumption about them.” Iblis had a ظَنّ - a conviction, an assumption - about humanity: that they would not be grateful, that they would not be patient, that they could be led astray.

فَٱتَّبَعُوهُ - “So they ended up following him.” They followed Shaytan’s whispers toward greed, ingratitude and economic oppression.

إِلَّا فَرِيقًا مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - “Except for a group among the true believers.” Even there, among that nation, there was a group that remained faithful. Some scholars suggest this refers to those who eventually became the Muslim community from among them, including those who were People of the Book at that time.

Ayah 21

وَمَا كَانَ لَهُۥ عَلَيْهِم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍ إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِمَّنْ هُوَ مِنْهَا فِى شَكٍّ ۗ وَرَبُّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَفِيظٌ

وَمَا كَانَ لَهُۥ عَلَيْهِم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍ - “And he did not have any authority against them at all.” Even the people who followed Shaytan - he had no actual power to control them. He couldn’t force them to make those choices.

إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ - “Except that We wanted to see who truly believes in the Akhirah” - who genuinely believes in the afterlife and accountability.

مِمَّنْ هُوَ مِنْهَا فِى شَكٍّ - “And who among them is in doubt about it.” Who has become a materialist, concerned only with this worldly life and its gains, doubting or disbelieving in what comes after?

وَرَبُّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَفِيظٌ - “And your Master is in complete guardianship over all things.” Allah ﷻ is الحَفِيظ - the ultimate Guardian and Protector. Had Allah ﷻ wanted, the flood would never have happened. Those kingdoms would have survived. Had He wanted, Sulaiman (AS) would not have died. There are rises and falls, the building of kingdoms and their destruction. As a result of these two downfalls - the Kingdom of Sulaiman (AS) and the Kingdom of Saba - Makkah became the trade alternative.

Trade started flowing through Makkah, which had been completely overlooked before because of Yemen’s dominance. The Yemenis felt they had been pushed aside. Later, when Abrahah became king in Yemen, he wanted to bring attention and prosperity back to his land as it had been in the old days. His plan was to destroy the Ka’bah and build a grand structure in Yemen to redirect the Arabs’ focus back to his territory. Behind his religious facade was pure economic ambition - he wanted to restore Yemen’s position as the center of Arabian trade.

Ayah 22

قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا لَهُمْ فِيهِمَا مِن شِرْكٍ وَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْهُم مِّن ظَهِيرٍ

Now the surah shifts to address the Quraysh directly about their worship of false deities.

قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ - “Say: Call upon those you claim to worship other than Allah.” زَعَمْتُم indicates a false assumption, a baseless claim.

لَا يَمْلِكُونَ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - “They don’t even own the weight of a speck, neither in the skies nor on the earth.” Earlier in the surah, Allah ﷻ said they won’t bear the weight of a speck on Judgment Day. Now He says they don’t even own the weight of a speck. They have zero ownership, zero authority.

وَمَا لَهُمْ فِيهِمَا مِن شِرْكٍ - “And they don’t have any partnership in them either” - neither in the skies nor the earth. They’re not partners with Allah ﷻ in creation or sovereignty.

وَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْهُم مِّن ظَهِيرٍ - “And He doesn’t have anyone from among them as a supporter or backer.” Allah ﷻ doesn’t need these false deities to help Him manage the universe.

Ayah 23

وَلَا تَنفَعُ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا لِمَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُۥ ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فُزِّعَ عَن قُلُوبِهِمْ قَالُوا۟ مَاذَا قَالَ رَبُّكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْكَبِيرُ

وَلَا تَنفَعُ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا لِمَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُۥ - “And no intercession works before Him except for the one He gives permission to.” Even the angels they worship - even they cannot intercede without Allah’s permission.

Now comes a remarkable description of the reality of these angels: حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فُزِّعَ عَن قُلُوبِهِمْ - “Until when the terror is removed from their hearts.”

The word فُزِّعَ is fascinating linguistically. In Arabic, when you add a shadda and a kasra to certain verbs, it indicates removal. For example, بَشَرَ means the peel, but the form قَشَّرَ means to remove the peel. Similarly, فَزَعَ means to be terrified, but فُزِّعَ means to have the terror removed.

This ayah tells us that whenever Allah ﷻ gives a command, every single time, the angels experience terror. Why? Because they understand the weight and authority of His words. When a command comes from the highest heaven, the angels in the lower ranks feel a tremendous sense of awe and fear.

قَالُوا۟ مَاذَا قَالَ رَبُّكُمْ - Then they ask, “What did your Master say?” They ask the angels in the higher ranks: “Has some commandment been given? We felt the terror down here. We were shaken. Is some nation about to be destroyed?”

قَالُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ - “They said: He declared the truth” - He revealed what is absolutely true and just.

وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْكَبِيرُ - “And He is the Ultimately High, the All-Great.”

This description is mentioned here for profound reasons. Two kingdoms were just discussed - the kingdom of Sulaiman (AS) and the kingdom of Saba. Now Allah ﷻ mentions His own kingdom, and how when He passes a verdict, how utterly terrified His subjects are. His first subjects are the angels themselves, and even they tremble at His commands.

This also addresses two types of shirk that existed among the Arabs. The first theology said: “These angels and idols will intercede for us with Allah ﷻ. They have connections with Him that we don’t have.”

The second theology claimed: “There’s a Major God with a capital G, but He has sub-gods that do His work because He can’t get Himself busy with all these details. So He made a god for the sun, a god for water, a god for trees, and they do His work. He needs them just like a government needs departments.”

In Islam, revelation does teach us that Allah ﷻ has appointed angels for certain tasks - the Angel of Death, the Angel of Revelation, angels that protect us. There are angels assigned different responsibilities. But when does this become shirk? It becomes shirk when people believe these angels are needed by Allah ﷻ, that they have independent power, and that you should call upon them directly. “Since the Angel of Death is in charge of death, I should call on him to cause someone’s death or to save me from death.”

In our din, all of these angels are complete slaves to Allah ﷻ. He doesn’t need them at all. He can intervene directly Himself. He created these angels to help us appreciate His power and His perfect organization of the universe. There are angels assigned to drag the hellfire, angels who are guardians of hellfire, angels who serve as greeters at the gates of Paradise. Angels have been given these different jobs, but these angels themselves have absolutely no independent power. In fact, when Allah ﷻ gives a command, they experience complete terror. Only when the shock wears off do they dare to ask, “What did He say? Is there something for us to do?” The command flows from one rank of angels to the next, and they must ask to understand their role.

This is the clarification being provided here about the true nature of angels and the absolute sovereignty of Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 24

۞ قُلْ مَن يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَإِنَّآ أَوْ إِيَّاكُمْ لَعَلَىٰ هُدًى أَوْ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ

قُلْ مَن يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ - “Say: Who provides for you from the skies and the earth?”

Provision is specifically mentioned because two kingdoms enjoyed great provision, and now the Quraysh enjoy provision. Where do you think yours comes from? Where did theirs come from? How are you any different from them?

قُلِ ٱللَّهُ - “Tell them: It’s Allah ﷻ.” He alone is the Provider.

وَإِنَّآ أَوْ إِيَّاكُمْ لَعَلَىٰ هُدًى أَوْ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ - “And no doubt, either we or you are upon guidance, or drowning in obvious misguidance.”

Allah ﷻ has now given them a complete historical perspective. How did you end up where you are? How do you enjoy the economic prosperity that you do? If the Kingdom of Sulaiman (AS) had survived, you would be worthless - they would be in charge. If the Kingdom of Saba had survived, you would be worthless - they would control the trade routes. Nobody would come to you. Nobody would recognize your value.

You’re enjoying this prosperity because Allah ﷻ created these kingdoms, allowed them to rise, and then brought about their downfall. You are now the inheritors of all these historical events. So where do you think your blessings come from?

Ayah 25

قُل لَّا تُسْـَٔلُونَ عَمَّآ أَجْرَمْنَا وَلَا نُسْـَٔلُ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ

This is a remarkable shift in approach. قُل لَّا تُسْـَٔلُونَ عَمَّآ أَجْرَمْنَا - “Tell them: You will not be asked about whatever crimes we have committed.”

Notice the word choice. The “crime” is attributed to the Muslims themselves, speaking from the disbelievers’ point of view. You think we’re the ones doing something criminal by following this message? Fine. You should know that you won’t be asked about what we did.

وَلَا نُسْـَٔلُ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ - “And we will not be asked about what you are doing.”

Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “the crimes you’re committing” because the time for that direct confrontation hasn’t come yet. He wants them to think without this becoming an aggressive, confrontational argument. If He had said “your crimes,” they would immediately become defensive: “What do you mean, crimes? We haven’t done any crimes!”

Instead, Allah ﷻ wants to call them to reflect on history, on their own situation, on the source of their provision - to think deeply without their pride getting in the way.

Ayah 26

قُلْ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنَا رَبُّنَا ثُمَّ يَفْتَحُ بَيْنَنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَهُوَ ٱلْفَتَّاحُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

قُلْ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنَا رَبُّنَا - “Tell them: Our Master will bring us together” - meaning the gathering will happen on the Day of Judgment.

ثُمَّ يَفْتَحُ بَيْنَنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ - “Then He will open between us with truth” - He will make His final decision, His ultimate verdict, with perfect justice. The word يَفْتَحُ means to open, and here it refers to opening up the final judgment, making the ultimate decision that resolves all disputes.

وَهُوَ ٱلْفَتَّاحُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ - “And He is the Ultimate Opener, the All-Knowing.” الْفَتَّاحُ - the One who opens all matters, who makes the final decisions. He knows everything - every hidden motive, every secret action, every truth - and His judgment will be absolutely perfect.

Ayah 27

قُلْ أَرُونِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَلْحَقْتُم بِهِۦ شُرَكَآءَ ۖ كَلَّا ۚ بَلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

The final challenge: قُلْ أَرُونِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَلْحَقْتُم بِهِۦ شُرَكَآءَ - “Tell them: Show me these partners that you have joined together with Him.”

أَلْحَقْتُم - you’ve attached, you’ve joined as partners with Allah ﷻ. What have you actually joined as partners with Him? What can you point to?

كَلَّا - “Absolutely not!” You cannot show me anything of substance. You’re not able to point to anything real.

بَلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ - “Rather, He is Allah, the Ultimate Authority, the All-Wise.”

What are you going to point at? Rocks? Statues? Angels who themselves tremble before Him? None of these have any power or authority. It is Allah ﷻ alone who possesses all might, all wisdom, all sovereignty. He is العَزِيز - the Almighty who cannot be overcome, and الحَكِيم - the All-Wise whose every decision and decree flows from perfect wisdom.

Ayah 28–40 · linked video: 05. Saba (Ayah 28-40)

Surah Saba: Ayahs 28-40

Ayah 28

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا كَآفَّةً لِّلنَّاسِ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

Allah ﷻ declares the universal nature of the Prophet’s ﷺ mission in unmistakable terms. The phrase كَآفَّةً لِّلنَّاسِ emphasizes that not a single nation or people has been excluded from this message. The linguistic construction itself reinforces this universality—the word كَآفَّةً means “all together,” and when combined with the prolonged syllables in tajweed recitation, it creates an effect of drawing out the meaning, as if to say: look around, examine every corner of humanity, and you will find that this messenger was sent for them all.

The Aborigines in Australia, the peoples in the depths of India, the tribes of Africa—Muhammad ﷺ is their messenger too. This is not a tribal message or a regional reform movement. It transcends every ethnic boundary, every geographic limitation, every cultural barrier.

The ayah describes the Prophet ﷺ as بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا—a giver of good news and a warner. Notice the sequence: good news comes first, then warning. This corresponds beautifully with how Surah Saba began, rooted in Allah’s ﷻ mercy. The glad tidings precede the caution, just as mercy precedes wrath in the divine nature.

Yet the ayah concludes with a sobering reality: وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ. The verb يَعْلَمُونَ suggests that people don’t know yet—there’s still time for them to learn. This remains our reality today. Most people on this planet still don’t truly know who Muhammad ﷺ was, why he was sent, or what message he brought.

Consider this: Muslims make up roughly one-fifth of the world’s population. If each Muslim simply shared this message with four people, imagine the transformation. This is our responsibility, our job description as inheritors of this message. If we fulfilled this basic duty, those who reject Islam would become the clear minority, because most people, if they truly understood, would embrace the truth.

Ayah 29

وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

The disbelievers shift tactics now, moving from rejection to mockery. Their question drips with sarcasm: “When is this promise of yours going to be fulfilled, if you’re really telling the truth?” They’re referring to the Day of Judgment, the ultimate accountability that the Quran keeps warning them about. Their tone suggests they don’t expect an answer—they’re trying to undermine the credibility of the message itself by pointing to what they perceive as delayed consequences.

Ayah 30

قُل لَّكُم مِّيعَادُ يَوْمٍ لَّا تَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ عَنْهُ سَاعَةً وَلَا تَسْتَقْدِمُونَ

Allah ﷻ instructs His messenger ﷺ to respond with certainty and precision. The word مِيعَاد is particularly powerful—it means a special appointment, a serious meeting that cannot be cancelled or rescheduled. This isn’t a casual get-together; this is an appointment set by Allah ﷻ Himself.

The ayah emphasizes the absolute precision of this timing: لَا تَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ عَنْهُ سَاعَةً وَلَا تَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (you will not delay it by even a moment, nor will you be able to bring it forward). Not even by ساعة—a brief moment. Human beings have no control over when this appointment occurs. You cannot postpone it when it arrives, and you cannot rush it to happen before its time. It will come exactly when Allah ﷻ has decreed it.

Ayah 31

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ وَلَا بِٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ ۗ وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ مَوْقُوفُونَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يَرْجِعُ بَعْضُهُمْ إِلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ٱلْقَوْلَ يَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أَنتُمْ لَكُنَّا مُؤْمِنِينَ

The disbelievers make their position clear: “We will never believe in this Quran, nor in what came before it.” The phrase وَلَا بِٱلَّذِى بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ refers to the previous scriptures, particularly the Torah. The Quraysh had heard fragments of Jewish teachings and noticed the similarities between what the Jews spoke about and what was in the Quran—a messenger coming, the afterlife, divine accountability. Their conspiracy theory? These two groups must be working together. “We reject both your book and theirs.”

But notice the irony embedded in their statement. While they’re trying to discredit the Quran, they’re actually affirming that it confirms the previous scriptures. Even in their rejection, they bear witness to its authenticity. The Quran validates what came before it, and even enemies can see this—yet the very rabbis of Bani Israel who should recognize this confirmation choose to deny it.

Then comes one of the most haunting phrases in the Quran: وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ—“If you could only see.” This phrase appears multiple times in the Quran, and it’s never completed. It’s always left hanging, trailing off into silence. Like when someone says, “If you had only been there…” or “If you had just called…” The unfinished sentence carries the weight of profound disappointment, of opportunities missed, of consequences that could have been avoided.

If you could only see the moment when the wrongdoers are made to stand before their Master. The scene unfolds with vivid detail: they’re turning on each other, trading accusations back and forth. Those who were weak and oppressed in worldly life—the followers, the masses—speak up to those who were arrogant and powerful: “If it weren’t for you, we would have been believers!” They blame the leaders, the elite, the propaganda machines that led them astray. “Your policies, your media, your systems kept us from Islam.”

Ayah 32

قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوٓا۟ أَنَحْنُ صَدَدْنَـٰكُمْ عَنِ ٱلْهُدَىٰ بَعْدَ إِذْ جَآءَكُم ۖ بَلْ كُنتُم مُّجْرِمِينَ

The arrogant fire back with cold logic: “Did we prevent you from guidance after it came to you?” Their defense is chilling in its simplicity—they’re right in a way. They didn’t physically stop anyone. No one locked the mosque doors. No one put chains on people to keep them from prayer. “Rather, you were criminals yourselves.”

This exchange mirrors real dynamics we see today. People in the Muslim world might blame Western influences: “You brought us MTV, Hollywood, secularism—you took us away from Islam!” And the response would be: “Did we put a lock on your mosques? Did we prevent you from going to prayer? You’re the ones who brought casinos to the Arab lands. You could have just walked across the street to the mosque, but you chose to walk into our establishments instead. Don’t blame us for your choices.”

Ayah 33

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ بَلْ مَكْرُ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ إِذْ تَأْمُرُونَنَآ أَن نَّكْفُرَ بِٱللَّهِ وَنَجْعَلَ لَهُۥٓ أَندَادًا ۚ وَأَسَرُّوا۟ ٱلنَّدَامَةَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلْأَغْلَـٰلَ فِىٓ أَعْنَاقِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

The weak refuse to accept full blame. They speak up again, this time pointing to something more insidious: “Rather, it was your schemes night and day.” The phrase مَكْرُ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ (the plotting of night and day) captures the relentless, 24/7 nature of modern propaganda and media influence. Your messaging never stopped. You were constantly in our minds, commanding us—sometimes explicitly, sometimes through subtle suggestion—to disbelieve in Allah ﷻ and to set up rivals besides Him.

These rivals aren’t just idols of stone. They’re our own desires, our obsessions, our consumer culture. We became worshippers of our own selves, slaves to our whims. Modern advertising understands this better than we think. Marketing firms literally study human behavior the way shepherds study sheep, understanding exactly which buttons to press to make people consume.

One of the most disturbing manifestations of this is found in internet statistics. Muslim-majority countries rank among the highest consumers of online filth. On Judgment Day, when this content is brought up, there will be this debate: “You produced it!” “But you downloaded it! There were plenty of other things available online—what were you doing?”

As both sides realize the full horror of their situation, they try to hide their shame and humiliation. Both the propaganda machines and their consumers see the punishment, and shackles are placed on the necks of those who disbelieved. The ayah ends with a rhetorical question that needs no answer: هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (Are they being recompensed with anything except what they used to do?). Both parties—the weak and the arrogant, the followers and the leaders—are wrongdoers in this scene. Both bear responsibility.

Ayah 34

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا فِى قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّذِيرٍ إِلَّا قَالَ مُتْرَفُوهَآ إِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ

Allah ﷻ now reveals a pattern that repeats throughout human history. Every time a warner was sent to any town, a specific group opposed him: the مُتْرَفُون—those immersed in luxury and blessing.

The word مُتْرَف is profound. It comes from تَرَف, meaning luxury and blessing. But مُتْرَف isn’t just someone wealthy—it’s someone who has become so consumed by their comfortable lifestyle that they’ve lost sight of reality. They live in a bubble of luxury: their entertainment, their devices, their comforts, their routines of consumption. Their entire existence revolves around the next episode, the next purchase, the next meal, the next distraction. They never evolve beyond this state.

These are the ones who always say: إِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ (We reject what you’ve been sent with). This rejection isn’t just about denying Islam wholesale. It’s more subtle today. Every time the message of Islam asks them to compromise their lifestyle, they respond: “I think you have a really harsh interpretation of Islam. We need an interpretation that doesn’t require me to change my comfortable life. What’s so wrong with this or that anyway?”

Consider how many blogs, forums, and Q&A sessions across the Muslim world are filled with variations of this question: “Is this really haram? Can I do it? Because I really want to do it. It can’t be that bad, right?” People seek religious opinions not to find the truth, but to find permission to continue their lifestyle unchanged. They want someone to tell them it’s okay to remain in their luxury without having to submit to what Allah ﷻ actually requires.

Ayah 35

وَقَالُوا۟ نَحْنُ أَكْثَرُ أَمْوَٰلًا وَأَوْلَـٰدًا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ

The wealthy elite make their logic explicit: “We have far more wealth and children than you. We’re clearly better off.” Then comes their fatal conclusion: “And we will never be punished.”

Their reasoning follows a simple but flawed line: God gave us all this wealth, this power, these blessings. That must mean He loves us. That must mean He’s pleased with us. How could He possibly want to punish us? Look at these other nations struggling with poverty, floods, bankruptcy, and debt—those are the people God wants to punish, not us. Our prosperity is proof of divine favor.

This delusion appears elsewhere in the Quran. In Surah Al-Kahf, it’s addressed at the individual level through the parable of the two men with gardens. Here in Surah Saba, it’s addressed at the national level. Entire nations convince themselves: “We’re the blessed nation. Why would we be punished? We enjoy such prosperity—that’s evidence that God is on our side.”

Ayah 36

قُلْ إِنَّ رَبِّى يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

Allah ﷻ instructs His messenger ﷺ to shatter this delusion with a fundamental truth: “Indeed, my Master expands provisions for whom He wills, and He restricts it for whom He wills.”

This puts everything in perspective. If these people have the right to claim divine favor because of their wealth, what about the nations described earlier in this surah? The people of Saba who had iron turn soft in their hands, who had endless gardens on both sides, who built civilizations of unprecedented sophistication—where are they now? Do you have anything comparable to what they had?

The distribution of wealth has nothing to do with Allah’s ﷻ pleasure or displeasure. He expands provisions for some and restricts them for others according to wisdom we may not fully comprehend. But most people don’t understand this reality.

Ayah 37

وَمَآ أَمْوَٰلُكُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُكُم بِٱلَّتِى تُقَرِّبُكُمْ عِندَنَا زُلْفَىٰٓ إِلَّا مَنْ ءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ جَزَآءُ ٱلضِّعْفِ بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَهُمْ فِى ٱلْغُرُفَـٰتِ ءَامِنُونَ

The truth is stated plainly: وَمَآ أَمْوَٰلُكُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُكُم (It is not your wealth, nor your children) that bring you close to Us in rank and status. Not at all.

Notice the structure of the ayah. When addressing those immersed in materialism and pride, Allah ﷻ speaks in the plural—addressing the collective, the society, the nation absorbed in this wrong attitude. But when He mentions the righteous, the language shifts to singular: إِلَّا مَنْ ءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا (except the one who believes and does righteous deeds).

This shift is profound. Within that society heading in the wrong direction, there stands the individual who chooses differently. Against all the pressure, against the current, against the norm, this person decides to live righteously. All the girls in school dress one way; she dresses as she’s supposed to. All the boys brag about certain things; he stays away from that behavior. Even children face these pressures—ask them what their classmates brag about, and you’ll be shocked at what has become normal in elementary school.

These individuals swimming upstream, maintaining their faith while everyone around them goes the opposite direction—they receive double reward. Why double? First, because they did what they were supposed to do. Second, because they did it while under pressure, when everyone else was heading another way. When you’re the only one standing for what’s right, you become the role model. Your example has weight beyond just your own deeds.

This echoes the previous surah’s discussion of double punishment and double reward for the mothers of the believers. They received double because they were role models. Similarly, when you stand up for righteousness in an environment where no one else does, you become that role model for others.

The reward is described beautifully: فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ جَزَآءُ ٱلضِّعْفِ بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ (for them is the reward of double for what they did). And then: وَهُمْ فِى ٱلْغُرُفَـٰتِ ءَامِنُونَ (and they will be in elevated chambers, secure).

The mention of security (آمِنُونَ) is deliberate. Why emphasize that they’ll be secure? Because right now, they’re not secure. They may have to compromise things. They may not be able to live as luxuriously as they want. They may have to cut back on certain comforts. They may constantly hear criticism from people, never feeling safe from harsh words and judgment. But in Paradise, all of that is removed. All those bad feelings disappear. Complete security, complete peace.

Ayah 38

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَسْعَوْنَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا مُعَـٰجِزِينَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ مُحْضَرُونَ

In stark contrast, those who strive against Our signs, trying to make them seem powerless or invalid—they will be brought into the punishment. The word مُعَـٰجِزِينَ suggests trying to render something incapable, trying to show that these signs have no effect, no power. They exert effort to undermine, to mock, to disprove. Their destination: brought before the punishment itself.

Ayah 39

قُلْ إِنَّ رَبِّى يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَقْدِرُ لَهُۥ ۚ وَمَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَهُوَ يُخْلِفُهُۥ ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ

This ayah returns to the theme of provision but adds a transformative principle about spending. Allah ﷻ repeats: my Master expands provisions for whom He wills among His slaves, and restricts it for whom He wills. But then comes the crucial addition: وَمَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَهُوَ يُخْلِفُهُۥ (whatever you spend of anything, He will replace it).

The word يُخْلِفُهُۥ means “He replaces it” or “He brings it back around.” This isn’t just about future reward in Paradise—though that’s certainly included. This ayah promises that whatever you spend for good, you will see its consequences and benefits returned to you in this very world.

When you spend in the path of goodness, some trouble headed your way changes course. Some problem that was going to strike you doesn’t happen. Some opportunity that would have brought chaos into your life—Allah ﷻ makes sure that job interview doesn’t go well, steering you away from harm. You might feel disappointed when things don’t work out the way you hoped, but Allah ﷻ is protecting you from something you cannot see. عَسَىٰٓ أَن تَكْرَهُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ (Perhaps you dislike something, but it is good for you).

This is what sadaqah does—it creates unseen ripples of benefit and protection in your life. Conversely, when you spend on evil, when you spend even a single penny in the wrong direction, its consequences, its spiritual damage, its curse comes back to you as well.

Remarkably, even the pre-Islamic Quraysh understood this principle to some degree. When the Ka’bah was damaged by flooding and needed reconstruction, they held a fundraiser. They refused to accept money from gambling, from prostitution, from alcohol sales. Their reasoning? “We want to build the Ka’bah with pure money, because if it’s built with corrupt money, corruption will follow around it.”

Think about this: these weren’t Muslims yet, but they had some moral understanding that the source of money matters, that spending from impure sources has consequences.

Apply this to our communities today. When we build mosques with money from interest-based loans, when liquor store owners donate large sums to ease their guilt from a year of earning haram income, when that poisoned money goes into the bricks and foundation of the masjid—then we shouldn’t be surprised when we see years of infighting, when leadership constantly changes and no one wants to stay, when the youth are disconnected, when the mosque looks more like a museum than a living spiritual center. We wonder: why is there no barakah here? Where is the blessing? The answer lies in وَمَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَهُوَ يُخْلِفُهُۥ—whatever you spend comes back to you.

This isn’t limited to money alone. When you spend your time well, when you sacrifice your comfort and peace of mind for something good, Allah ﷻ returns it to you in forms of peace, health, and other blessings you might not even recognize as returns on your investment. The ayah concludes: وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ (And He is the Best of Providers). He provides not just material sustenance, but every form of goodness in your life.

Ayah 40

وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ يَقُولُ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِيَّاكُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْبُدُونَ

The surah moves toward its conclusion by depicting a scene from the Day of Judgment. Allah ﷻ will gather them all together—every soul that ever lived. Then He will turn to the angels with a question that requires no answer, a question designed to expose the absurdity and disgrace of what happened in worldly life: “Was it you that these people were worshipping?”

This question hangs in the air, and though the transcript ends here, we know from the continuation of the surah how the angels will respond—with complete innocence and horror at the suggestion. They will declare their distance from such worship, affirming that these people worshipped only their own delusions.

The ayah sets the stage for ultimate accountability, where every false claim, every misplaced devotion, every act of shirk will be laid bare before all of creation. No excuses will hold. No rationalizations will work. The truth will be undeniable, and everyone will see clearly what they spent their lives pursuing, and whether it was worth the price they paid.

Ayah 40–54 · linked video: 06. Saba (Ayah 40-54)

Ayah 40

وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ يَقُولُ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِيَّاكُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْبُدُونَ

And on that Day He will gather them all together, then He will say to the angels, “Was it you that these people used to worship?”

Allah ﷻ paints a scene from the Day of Judgment where a remarkable confrontation takes place. The disbelievers who associated partners with Allah ﷻ will be gathered alongside those they claimed to worship. Allah ﷻ then addresses the angels directly, asking whether these people were actually worshiping them.

This divine question serves multiple purposes. It exposes the false claims of those who attributed divinity to angels, and it gives the angels themselves an opportunity to declare their innocence and their complete submission to Allah ﷻ alone. The question format itself carries a powerful rhetorical force, as Allah ﷻ already knows the answer, but the public declaration serves to establish truth and justice before all of creation.

Ayah 41

قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ أَنتَ وَلِيُّنَا مِن دُونِهِم ۖ بَلْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْبُدُونَ ٱلْجِنَّ ۖ أَكْثَرُهُم بِهِم مُّؤْمِنُونَ
