They will say, “Glory be to You! You are our Protector, not them. Rather, they used to worship the jinn; most of them were believers in them.”

The angels respond with سُبْحَـٰنَكَ, immediately declaring Allah’s ﷻ perfection and distancing themselves from any claim of divinity. They affirm that Allah ﷻ is their وَلِيّ (protector and guardian), not these disbelievers. The angels completely reject any association with the worship directed toward them.

Then comes the striking revelation: بَلْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْبُدُونَ ٱلْجِنَّ (rather, they used to worship the jinn). While the disbelievers thought they were calling upon angels, it was actually the jinn who were responding to them, deceiving them through whispers and manipulation. The jinn would whisper into their ears, influencing their religious practices and leading them to create elaborate rituals and beliefs.

Consider how this manifests in various religious traditions. When people call upon intermediaries or make statues weep, or when they witness seemingly miraculous events that strengthen their false beliefs, it is often the jinn orchestrating these deceptions. The angels, if present, would never participate in such acts of shirk, even though they have the power to intervene. They remain obedient to Allah’s ﷻ command, watching as people commit these offenses against the very beings they claim to honor.

The phrase أَكْثَرُهُم بِهِم مُّؤْمِنُونَ (most of them were believers in them) reveals that these people had genuine conviction in the jinn’s power, even without realizing the true nature of what they were worshiping. They were sincere in their false beliefs, making their misguidance even more tragic.

Ayah 42

فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا يَمْلِكُ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ نَّفْعًا وَلَا ضَرًّا وَنَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُم بِهَا تُكَذِّبُونَ

So on that Day, none of you will possess any power to benefit or harm one another. And We will say to those who wronged themselves, “Taste the punishment of the Fire which you used to deny.”

The word يَمْلِكُ means to own but also to control. On the Day of Judgment, all the relationships and dependencies of the worldly life dissolve completely. No one will have any control over benefit or harm for anyone else. Notice the order: نَفْعًا (benefit) comes before ضَرًّا, perhaps indicating that people’s desperate hope for benefit will be shattered first, followed by their inability to ward off harm.

Allah ﷻ then addresses those who wronged themselves directly, commanding them to taste the punishment. The verb ذُوقُوا۟ (taste) is deliberate, as tasting is the most intimate way of experiencing something. They must fully experience what they denied. The Fire is described as ٱلَّتِى كُنتُم بِهَا تُكَذِّبُونَ (that which you used to completely deny), emphasizing their absolute rejection using the intensive form تُكَذِّبُونَ, which indicates persistent, emphatic denial.

Ayah 43

وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ قَالُوا۟ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا رَجُلٌ يُرِيدُ أَن يَصُدَّكُمْ عَمَّا كَانَ يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُكُمْ وَقَالُوا۟ مَا هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ إِفْكٌ مُّفْتَرًى ۚ وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ

And when Our clear verses are recited to them, they say, “This is nothing but a man who wants to turn you away from what your fathers used to worship.” And they say, “This is nothing but an invented lie.” And when the truth comes to them, the disbelievers say, “This is nothing but obvious magic.”

Earlier in the surah, the disbelievers’ objections were less aggressive. They wondered if perhaps something was wrong with the Messenger ﷺ, questioning whether he might have a jinn possessing him. But as time progresses, their rhetoric escalates and hardens.

When confronted with بَيِّنَـٰتٍ (absolutely clear, miraculous signs), they resort to three distinct attacks. First, they frame the message politically, painting the Prophet ﷺ as someone with an agenda to destroy their heritage. “Don’t you love your father? Don’t you respect your traditions? This man wants to dishonor your ancestors’ memory and destroy our lifestyle!” This is a patriotic appeal, accusing him of being a traitor to the nation and its value system.

Second, they escalate to direct insult: إِفْكٌ مُّفْتَرًى (a vicious, invented lie). This is aggressive dismissal meant to discredit without engaging the message’s content.

Third, and most revealing, they call it سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (obvious magic). Here, calling the Quran magic is actually an unconscious admission of its power. When someone calls something magic, they’re acknowledging they cannot explain its effect, that it has overwhelming influence they cannot rationally account for. They’re admitting the Quran’s power over hearts while desperately trying to warn people away from that power.

Notice how these accusations progress: political threat, outright lie, and finally, an admission wrapped in denial. Each rhetorical strategy reveals the disbelievers’ inability to genuinely confront the truth of the message itself.

Ayah 44

وَمَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم مِّن كُتُبٍ يَدْرُسُونَهَا ۖ وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمْ قَبْلَكَ مِن نَّذِيرٍ

We have not given them any Books to study, nor have We sent them any warner before you.

The Makkans were an illiterate society. Allah ﷻ points out that they have no scriptural tradition to refer to, no previous Books they studied that would give them grounds for their objections. They have no revealed knowledge upon which to base their rejection of the Quran. Furthermore, no previous warner had been sent to them specifically before the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

This ayah dismantles any claim to religious authority they might make. Their rejection isn’t based on careful study of previous revelation or competing divine guidance. It’s based purely on adherence to inherited tradition without divine foundation.

Ayah 45

وَكَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ وَمَا بَلَغُوا۟ مِعْشَارَ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ فَكَذَّبُوا۟ رُسُلِى ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ

Those before them also denied, and these people have not attained even one-tenth of what We had given them, yet they denied My messengers. So how terrible was My punishment!

Allah ﷻ reminds the Makkans of previous nations who denied their messengers, such as the jinn in the time of Prophet Sulaiman (AS) and the people of Saba. The striking statement is: وَمَا بَلَغُوا۟ مِعْشَارَ (they haven’t even reached one-tenth). The Makkans, for all their pride in their status and city, possessed only a fraction of the wealth, power and civilization that previous nations enjoyed.

The question then becomes: what are you so arrogant about? Those nations, with far greater resources and strength, were destroyed when they persisted in denial. فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ (How was My punishment?) The word نَكِير actually means both a warning and something that becomes too horrible to describe. When Allah ﷻ destroys a nation, the devastation becomes indescribable. Witnesses cannot find words to capture what they saw. This rhetorical question hangs in the air as both warning and promise.

Ayah 46

۞ قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَعِظُكُم بِوَٰحِدَةٍ ۖ أَن تَقُومُوا۟ لِلَّهِ مَثْنَىٰ وَفُرَٰدَىٰ ثُمَّ تَتَفَكَّرُوا۟ ۚ مَا بِصَاحِبِكُم مِّن جِنَّةٍ ۚ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ لَّكُم بَيْنَ يَدَىْ عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ

Say, “I advise you to do only one thing: that you stand before Allah in pairs or individually, then reflect. Your companion is not possessed. He is only a warner to you of an approaching severe punishment.”

This is a remarkable and unique passage in the Quran. Allah ﷻ asks for just one thing: أَعِظُكُم بِوَٰحِدَةٍ (I counsel you regarding one matter). Just think about this one thing.

أَن تَقُومُوا۟ لِلَّهِ مَثْنَىٰ وَفُرَٰدَىٰ (Stand before Allah in pairs or individually). Allah ﷻ is addressing the phenomenon of groupthink, the herd mentality that prevents individual reflection. When people think as a group, they stop thinking for themselves and simply join the bandwagon.

Consider how groupthink develops. Someone says something slightly questionable. A second person agrees and exaggerates it slightly. A third person hears the exaggeration and takes it further. A fourth person joins and the matter snowballs completely out of proportion. What started as a minor observation becomes a major accusation through collective amplification.

This is precisely what happened with the Makkans’ response to the Prophet ﷺ. One person said he disagrees with their traditions. Another said he hates their traditions. A third called him a traitor. The rhetoric kept building and building until rational thought was completely abandoned.

Allah ﷻ says: leave that group mentality aside. Come individually, or perhaps two at a time—maybe a husband and wife, no more. Come and think for yourselves. When you remove yourself from the crowd’s influence and honestly reflect, what do you actually know about Muhammad ﷺ? Not what you’ve heard, but what is your direct experience with him?

مَا بِصَاحِبِكُم مِّن جِنَّةٍ (Your companion has no jinn possessing him). If you thought for yourself for just a moment, reflecting on what you actually know about him, you would come to the right conclusion. But you’re so immersed in collective rhetoric, so flooded with groupthink, that you cannot see clearly.

This counsel connects beautifully to one of the surah’s themes: the story of one believer standing against a nation indulged in luxury. That individual stood against the crowd. Allah ﷻ is now inviting the Makkans to do the same: stand against the crowd mentality and think for yourself.

إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ لَّكُم بَيْنَ يَدَىْ عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ (He is only a warner to you of an approaching severe punishment). The phrase بَيْنَ يَدَىْ (literally “between the hands of”) means right in front of, immediately before. The punishment is right around the corner, at the doorstep. The urgency in the Prophet’s ﷺ warning reflects the urgency of the impending consequence.

Ayah 47

قُلْ مَا سَأَلْتُكُم مِّنْ أَجْرٍ فَهُوَ لَكُمْ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ

Say, “Whatever reward I might have asked of you is yours to keep. My reward is only from Allah, and He is Witness over all things.”

The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to address another accusation: that his true motive is financial gain. It’s as if someone came to him with money, assuming that’s what he really wants, and he responds: “Keep it. That’s for you.”

Consider someone who appears to need help, sharing a long story about their struggles, but when you offer assistance, they simply take the money and walk away. You realize the entire conversation was just a pretext for a transaction. The Makkans were assuming the Prophet ﷺ was doing exactly this—using religious talk to eventually get something material from them.

The Prophet ﷺ cuts through this cynicism completely: فَهُوَ لَكُمْ (it is for you). إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ (My compensation is only from Allah). This principle is fundamental to Islamic teaching and leadership. Those who represent the religion, especially in public service, must go out of their way to establish this policy and stand by it firmly. Why? Because otherwise, this exact criticism gains weight: “They’re just after money.”

When someone invites you to give a talk or lead prayer, and they offer compensation, the principled response is refusal. If they insist because they have it in their budget, direct it to charity. If you need help with transportation costs, that’s different, but don’t mix acts of worship with financial transactions. Teaching can be compensated because it’s a professional service, but worship is worship. The Friday khutbah, the reminder, the spiritual counsel—these are acts of ibadah that should never be commodified.

When those who speak about Islam maintain this clear policy, their motives remain unquestionable. Their agenda becomes transparent. The message becomes powerful precisely because it’s obviously not about personal gain. The Prophet ﷺ establishes this principle most clearly: وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ (And He is Witness over all things). Allah ﷻ sees the sincerity, knows the intentions, and that is sufficient.

Ayah 48

قُلْ إِنَّ رَبِّى يَقْذِفُ بِٱلْحَقِّ عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ

Say, “Indeed, my Lord hurls the truth. He is the Knower of all unseen.”

The verb يَقْذِفُ is powerful—it means to throw something hard and far. Allah ﷻ doesn’t gently place the truth; He hurls it. This imagery suggests truth as something solid, substantial, forceful. In Surah Al-Anbiya, we learned: “We hurl the truth at falsehood, and it crushes its head, and falsehood vanishes.”

But here, Allah ﷻ doesn’t even mention falsehood as a target. Falsehood is so weak, so insignificant, it’s not even worthy of mention. He simply hurls the truth. The title عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ (Knower of all unseen) emphasizes that the One who controls all hidden realities is the same One launching this truth into the world. His knowledge encompasses everything concealed, so when He reveals truth, it comes from absolute, comprehensive knowledge.

Ayah 49

قُلْ جَآءَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَمَا يُبْدِئُ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ وَمَا يُعِيدُ

Say, “The truth has come, and falsehood can neither originate anything nor restore anything.”

جَآءَ ٱلْحَقُّ (Truth has arrived). It’s here, established, undeniable. وَمَا يُبْدِئُ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ (Falsehood cannot originate) وَمَا يُعِيدُ (nor can it restore or bring anything back).

This statement cuts to the heart of truth and falsehood’s relationship. Truth is always the original state. Everything began with truth. Falsehood never started anything; it’s always a corruption, a distortion of truth that already existed. Lies never create; they only twist what’s real. And falsehood cannot make a comeback—once truth is established, falsehood cannot restore its former position.

The finality of truth’s victory is embedded in this simple statement. It’s not a future promise; it’s a present reality: truth has come, and falsehood’s impotence is exposed.

Ayah 50

قُلْ إِن ضَلَلْتُ فَإِنَّمَآ أَضِلُّ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِى ۖ وَإِنِ ٱهْتَدَيْتُ فَبِمَا يُوحِىٓ إِلَىَّ رَبِّىٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ سَمِيعٌ قَرِيبٌ

Say, “If I am misguided, then I am only misguided to my own loss. But if I am guided, it is by what my Lord reveals to me. Indeed, He is All-Hearing, Ever Near.”

The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to put his own character and integrity on the line. This is a stunning demonstration of confidence in the truth. He says: إِن ضَلَلْتُ فَإِنَّمَآ أَضِلُّ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِى (If I am misguided, then I am only harming myself). If you can honestly say I’m misguided, then fine—I alone bear that consequence.

وَإِنِ ٱهْتَدَيْتُ فَبِمَا يُوحِىٓ إِلَىَّ رَبِّىٓ (But if I am committed to guidance, it is by what my Lord reveals to me). This is the key: if you know for a fact that I am committed to moral character, to decency, to truth, then understand that this commitment comes from my Master’s revelation. You can verify my character through your own experience with me. And if my character is sound, then you must acknowledge its source.

إِنَّهُۥ سَمِيعٌ قَرِيبٌ (He is All-Hearing, Ever Near). Allah ﷻ hears everything, knows the reality of the Prophet’s ﷺ message and mission. And He is قَرِيبٌ—very close. This closeness is both comfort and warning: comfort to the believer that Allah ﷻ is near in His knowledge and support, and warning to the denier that Allah ﷻ is near in His awareness of their rejection.

Ayah 51

وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذْ فَزِعُوا۟ فَلَا فَوْتَ وَأُخِذُوا۟ مِن مَّكَانٍ قَرِيبٍ

If only you could see when they are terrified, and there is no escape, and they are seized from a nearby place.

وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ (If only you could see)—again, this construction appears without an answer, creating powerful suspension. If you could only witness this scene…

إِذْ فَزِعُوا۟ (When they are terrified). فَزِعَ means to be so overwhelmed with fear that rational thought ceases. Their wits are completely gone; they cannot think straight because terror has seized them entirely.

فَلَا فَوْتَ (There is no escape). The word فَوْت means refuge, an exit, a way out. Here, there is absolutely no escape whatsoever. وَأُخِذُوا۟ مِن مَّكَانٍ قَرِيبٍ (And they are seized from a nearby place). No one is far from the authorities. When the angels come to execute Allah’s ﷻ command, when the entire reality is transformed, everyone is within reach. Some might think they’re far enough away to escape, but the ayah declares: everyone is near, everyone is within grasp. There is no distant safe haven.

Ayah 52

وَقَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦ وَأَنَّىٰ لَهُمُ ٱلتَّنَاوُشُ مِن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ

And they will say, “We believe in it!” But how can they reach it from a place so far away?

ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦ (We believe in it now! We believe!) The desperate declaration of faith comes too late. وَأَنَّىٰ لَهُمُ ٱلتَّنَاوُشُ (But how can they reach it?)

ٱلتَّنَاوُشُ is a beautiful, vivid word. It means to barely reach something at the very end of your arms’ reach—like when something is just beyond your grasp and you stretch as far as you can, fingers extended, barely touching it. Allah ﷻ describes these people as being dragged toward punishment, desperately reaching out toward what they imagine is a safe place far away.

مِن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (From a place far away). That safety they’re reaching for? It’s impossibly distant. They’re trying to grasp at something completely beyond their reach. Their faith, offered now, cannot reach back to the world they left behind. There’s no way to bridge that distance.

Ayah 53

وَقَدْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَيَقْذِفُونَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ مِن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ

When they had already rejected it before. And they used to cast aspersions at the unseen from a place far away.

وَقَدْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ مِن قَبْلُ (When they had already disbelieved in it before). Their current claim of faith is exposed as hollow—they spent their entire worldly lives in rejection.

وَيَقْذِفُونَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ (And they used to throw at the unseen). This phrase means they used to insult, mock and make jokes about the unseen realities—the Afterlife, Judgment Day, resurrection. They would talk trash about these realities dismissively.

مِن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (From a place far away). Back then, when they were in the world, the Afterlife seemed distant and unreal to them, so they felt safe mocking it. Now that distance has collapsed. What was far is now immediate, and the place they thought was near (the worldly life and its safety) is now impossibly far.

The contrast is devastating: they threw insults at the Afterlife from their “safe” distance in the world, and now they’re desperately reaching for that worldly safety from an unbridgeable distance in the Afterlife.

Ayah 54

وَحِيلَ بَيْنَهُمْ وَبَيْنَ مَا يَشْتَهُونَ كَمَا فُعِلَ بِأَشْيَاعِهِم مِّن قَبْلُ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ فِى شَكٍّ مُّرِيبٍۭ

And a barrier will be placed between them and what they desire, just as it was done with their counterparts before. Indeed, they were in a disquieting doubt.

وَحِيلَ بَيْنَهُمْ وَبَيْنَ مَا يَشْتَهُونَ (A barrier has been placed between them and what they desire). The passive construction emphasizes the action’s finality—the barrier simply exists now, placed by divine decree. Everything they craved, every temptation they lived for, every luxury and lust that defined their existence—all of it is now on the other side of an impenetrable wall.

كَمَا فُعِلَ بِأَشْيَاعِهِم مِّن قَبْلُ (Just as it was done with their counterparts before). أَشْيَاع means groups of like-minded people, their ideological ancestors. This same barrier was placed between previous nations of disbelievers and their desires. History repeats itself in the spiritual realm.

إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ فِى شَكٍّ مُّرِيبٍۭ (Indeed, they were in a disquieting doubt). This final phrase carries profound weight. شَكّ means doubt, and مُّرِيب means doubt-causing, doubt-spreading, disquieting. This describes people who weren’t just privately uncertain—their doubt was infectious. They spread it to others.

Notice how the surah began with these people’s doubts about the Prophet ﷺ: “Maybe he’s got a jinn? Does he make lies against Allah ﷻ?” They weren’t sure themselves, but the way they expressed these doubts caused confusion in others. “Yeah, maybe there isn’t truth in this message. Maybe Islam isn’t real.” Their doubts snowballed through the community, poisoning hearts and minds.

Allah ﷻ specifically condemns at the surah’s conclusion those who harbor doubt about Islam and don’t keep it to themselves, but actively spread it. They create an atmosphere of skepticism that leads others astray. The punishment described throughout these ayahs falls particularly on those who not only rejected truth themselves but worked to ensure others would reject it too.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from doubt that disturbs our hearts, and may He protect us from ever becoming a source of doubt that disturbs others. May He make us people who spread certainty, clarity and conviction in His truth.
